methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Chips & Semiconductors (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 14 same-sector peers exist in Chips & Semiconductors, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 20.3x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

data.narrative:
Ranovus is a Series D+ semiconductor company that has raised $236 million including a $36 million strategic investment fund grant in August 2025. The valuation analysis draws from a comp universe of 14 same-sector peers (3 public, 8 private with notable names including Cerebras, PsiQuantum, Etched, SambaNova Systems, and Credo Semiconductor), though the narrow-filter peer set proved insufficient for traditional triangulation.

The peer-median method is not applicable due to lack of sufficient same-stage and adjacent-stage comparables meeting quantitative thresholds—zero matches within the narrow filter, requiring a minimum of three datapoints. Public-comp anchoring cannot be applied because Ranovus's revenue is not disclosed, which is typical for private companies. The public semiconductor universe (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) trades at a median 20.3x price-to-sales, but this remains unusable without the target's top-line figures.

Given the absence of applicable quantitative triangulation methods, a defensible valuation range cannot be derived from the provided data. The analysis is constrained by two material gaps: insufficient private peer density at Ranovus's stage and the absence of revenue disclosure. Any valuation conclusion would require either (1) disclosure of Ranovus's revenue or near-term revenue guidance, or (2) identification of additional Series D+ semiconductor peers with disclosed valuations. The $236 million raised serves as a floor but does not itself imply enterprise value.
